summary: Joel Spolsky announces several product updates in a brief 'new stuff' post. He highlights SourceGear's Vault 1.1 shipping with FogBUGZ integration, CityDesk 2.0 reaching its fourth beta with improved HTML generation and hundreds of bug fixes, and a new plasma TV for the Fog Creek office. He outlines a release strategy for CityDesk: a polishing release (2.0) focused on fixing bugs for existing users, followed by a major feature release (3.0) later in the year.
key_points: SourceGear's Vault 1.1 is the first source control system with built-in FogBUGZ integration
CityDesk 2.0 is a deliberate 'polishing' release focused on fixing hundreds of bugs before adding major new features in 3.0
The CityDesk WYSIWYG editor now generates valid XHTML and preserves whitespace
Joel advocates fixing all small broken things before creating major new functionality
money_quotes: Our goal was to make CityDesk a much more satisfying product for our current users, people who are happy with 1.0 but could be made even happier with some key enhancements.
We've fixed literally hundreds of bugs and annoyances.
We have a compulsive need to fix all the little tiny broken things before we create major new areas of functionality.
After a few minutes I was impressed, after a few days I am in love with it.
But the Playstation is Not Tax Deductible.
key_insight: A polishing release that fixes hundreds of small bugs and annoyances should precede major new feature work, because getting the foundation right makes existing users happier and creates a stronger base for future development.
